speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Based on your recent visit and evaluation, I’m diagnosing you with essential hypertension, which we’ll manage carefully moving forward.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level came back at 161 g/L — that’s well within the normal range for an adult male.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, Doctor. I’ve been feeling generally okay, though I did notice some fatigue over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: I’ve prescribed Atorvastatin 20 mg to be taken once daily at night — this will help manage your cholesterol and support cardiovascular health.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. I started that last night. But actually, I had a bit of a fever this morning — my thermometer read 38.7°C.

speaker: doctor
text: Ah — yes, your temperature was elevated at 38.7°C during today’s assessment. That’s consistent with what you’re reporting.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate was stable at 79 bpm — not tachycardic, which is reassuring given the fever.

speaker: patient
text: That’s good to hear. My blood pressure was also checked earlier — it felt a little low, but I didn’t catch the numbers.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, your diastolic pressure was 64 mmHg — slightly lower than usual, though still within acceptable limits for now.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your ongoing cardiovascular risk profile and the fact that your LDL remains above target despite current dosing, I’m going to increase your Atorvastatin to 40 mg nightly.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll adjust that tonight. By the way — when I reviewed my lab results just now, I noticed my hemoglobin was listed as 144 g/L. That seemed lower than before.

speaker: doctor
text: You're right — that reading differs from the 161 g/L we saw earlier in the same lab session. Let me double-check the timing and specimen labels — sometimes duplicate draws can get mismatched.

speaker: doctor
text: After reviewing the lab logs, the 144 g/L appears to be from a repeat draw drawn 45 minutes after the first — likely affected by mild hemoconcentration from the fever. We’ll rely on the initial value of 161 g/L as the more accurate baseline.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. So the 161 g/L is the one we should go by — that’s the number from the first blood draw this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly. That’s the reference value for tracking — especially important since we’re monitoring for any statin-related hematologic effects, though those are rare.

speaker: patient
text: Right. I haven’t noticed bruising or unusual bleeding, but I’ll keep an eye out.

speaker: doctor
text: Good. Also, let’s monitor your temperature closely over the next 48 hours — if the fever persists beyond that or you develop chills or shortness of breath, please call us immediately.

speaker: patient
text: Will do. And just to confirm — I’m now on 40 mg of Atorvastatin every night, correct?

speaker: patient
text: Yes — and that 161 g/L is the hemoglobin value we discussed first, the one before the repeat test.

speaker: doctor
text: Correct — that’s the validated baseline. We’ll recheck it in six weeks along with liver enzymes and lipid panel. In the meantime, stay well-hydrated and avoid grapefruit juice.
